text:
ONCOLOGY NEW PATIENT CONSULTATION NOTE

Patient: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
Age: 58 years
Sex: Female
Referring Provider: Dermatology / Surgical Oncology
Reason for Consultation: Newly diagnosed Stage IV cutaneous melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant, with hepatic, lymph node, and subcutaneous metastases. Referral for systemic therapy planning.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old woman of West African and British descent who presents to the Medical Oncology clinic for initial consultation regarding a new diagnosis of metastatic cutaneous melanoma. She reports a several-month history of a rapidly enlarging mass in her left axilla, associated with an unintentional weight loss of approximately 8 pounds over the past two months, and intermittent discomfort in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu first noticed a growing lump in her left axilla approximately four months prior to this visit. She initially attributed it to a minor skin irritation but sought evaluation from her dermatologist after noticing the mass was firm and non-tender, and not responding to conservative measures. She had previously been followed by Dermatology for many years for dysplastic nevus syndrome, during which time she had undergone multiple excisions of atypical nevi from the trunk, back, and extremities. On routine dermatology follow-up, a pigmented lesion on her upper back was noted to have changed in character - it had become larger, darker, and developed irregular borders and surface ulceration. The lesion measured approximately 18 mm in greatest dimension and was excised with wide local margins; pathology confirmed a primary melanoma with Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V.

Subsequent workup for lymph node involvement and distant metastatic spread was initiated. A sentinel lymph node biopsy was not performed given the clinically and radiographically evident bulky left axillary disease; instead, the patient underwent excisional biopsy of the dominant left axillary lymph node, which measured 4.8 cm on imaging and was confirmed to harbor metastatic melanoma. Cross-sectional CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated bilateral axillary adenopathy (left side dominant at 4.8 x 3.5 cm with central necrosis), multiple hypodense hepatic lesions in segments IV, VI, and VII (largest 3.2 x 2.8 cm in segment VI), and three subcutaneous nodules along the left flank (up to 1.8 cm). No pulmonary nodules, osseous lesions, or intra-abdominal organ involvement were identified. MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium contrast showed no intracranial metastases and no leptomeningeal enhancement, with only an incidental stable 4 mm pineal cyst.

Molecular testing performed on the left axillary lymph node specimen by a comprehensive 500-gene solid tumor next generation sequencing panel revealed a BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%), which was also confirmed by BRAF V600E immunohistochemistry (VE1 antibody, 3+ staining). Additional pathogenic alterations included NF1 p.Q1300* nonsense mutation (VAF 52%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, TP53 p.R248W (VAF 41%), PTEN p.L108R (VAF 22%), TERT promoter C228T mutation (VAF 55%), and MAP2K1 amplification. No NRAS or KIT mutations were identified. Tumor mutational burden was high at 42 mutations/Mb. PD-L1 expression by IHC (22C3 pharmDx) showed a TPS of 30% and combined positive score (CPS) of 55.

She reports that the right upper quadrant discomfort is dull in nature, non-radiating, not associated with jaundice, nausea, or vomiting. She has noticed increased fatigue over the past several weeks and a significant decline in appetite. She denies any bone pain, neurological symptoms, visual changes, or new skin lesions beyond those already identified. She has not experienced fever, night sweats, or chills. She has noted mild dyspnea on exertion but no cough, hemoptysis, or pleuritic chest pain.

This is her first oncology consultation. She has not received any systemic cancer therapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Unintentional weight loss of ~8 lbs over 2 months. Fatigue, moderate. Appetite decreased. Denies fever or chills.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Denies headache, visual changes, epistaxis.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea, no cough, no hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, hematochezia, or melena.
Genitourinary: Denies dysuria, hematuria, urinary frequency.
Musculoskeletal: Denies bone pain, joint pain, myalgia.
Neurological: Denies headache, dizziness, focal weakness, sensory changes, or seizures.
Skin/Integumentary: Longstanding history of dysplastic nevi; multiple prior excisions. No new skin lesions reported beyond known disease.
Hematologic/Lymphatic: Palpable left axillary mass. Denies easy bruising or bleeding.
Endocrine: Denies polydipsia, polyuria, or heat/cold intolerance.
Psychiatric: Appropriate anxiety regarding new diagnosis. Denies depression or suicidal ideation.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Dysplastic nevus syndrome (congenital melanocytic nevus syndrome variant) - longstanding, multiple prior excisions of atypical nevi from trunk, back, and extremities over the past 20+ years; followed by Dermatology.
2. Hypertension - well-controlled on amlodipine.
3. Hypothyroidism - on levothyroxine (note: TSH was within normal limits at last check; see current labs).
4. Seasonal allergic rhinitis - managed with antihistamines PRN.
5. Primary melanoma of the back - recently excised; see above.

No prior malignancy other than current melanoma. No prior chemotherapy or radiation.

---

SURGICAL HISTORY

1. Multiple wide local excisions of atypical nevi (back, trunk, extremities) over many years.
2. Excision of primary melanoma, upper back, with wide local margins - recent.
3. Excisional biopsy of left axillary lymph node - recent.
4. Appendectomy in childhood.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

Maternal uncle: Cutaneous melanoma (age at diagnosis unknown; ultimately died of disease per patient report).
Father: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Mother: Breast cancer in her 60s (patient uncertain of subtype).
No known family history of pancreatic cancer, colon cancer, or other BRAF-related malignancies.
No known familial melanoma syndrome formally evaluated; given maternal uncle with melanoma and personal history of dysplastic nevus syndrome, genetic counseling referral considered.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Occupation: High school science teacher - currently on medical leave.
Smoking: Never-smoker.
Alcohol: Occasional social use, approximately 2-3 drinks per week.
Recreational drugs: Denies.
Marital status: Married; husband is present at today's visit and involved in care.
Children: Two adult children.
Residence: Lives at home with husband.
Performance status: ECOG 1 - ambulatory and capable of light activities; has noted some limitation due to fatigue but remains largely functional.
Sun exposure/tanning history: Patient recalls significant sun exposure during childhood in Ghana before immigrating; no tanning bed use. Has used SPF 50+ sunscreen and protective clothing for the past 15+ years per dermatology recommendation.

---

ALLERGIES

Sulfa antibiotics - rash (maculopapular, non-anaphylactic).
No known drug allergies to chemotherapy agents, contrast dye, or biologics. No latex allergy.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Levothyroxine 50 mcg orally once daily (hypothyroidism; last TSH was within normal limits)
3. Loratadine 10 mg orally once daily PRN (allergic rhinitis)
4. Vitamin D3 1000 IU orally once daily
5. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally once daily
6. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 82 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 64.2 kg, height 165 cm, BMI 23.6 kg/m².

General: Well-appearing, alert, and oriented. Mildly fatigued affect. No acute distress. Appropriate affect; understandably anxious about diagnosis.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no lesions.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy appreciated.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. No pleural rub.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft. Mild right upper quadrant tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rebound. No hepatomegaly appreciated on palpation (hepatic dullness normal on percussion). No splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.

Lymph Nodes:
- Left axilla: A firm, fixed, irregular mass measuring approximately 5 cm in greatest dimension. Overlying skin intact without erythema or fluctuance. Non-tender on palpation.
- Right axilla: No significant lymphadenopathy.
- Bilateral inguinal regions: No lymphadenopathy palpable.
- Cervical, supraclavicular, infraclavicular chains: No lymphadenopathy.

Skin/Integumentary: Multiple well-healed surgical scars on the back, trunk, and upper extremities consistent with prior excisions of dysplastic nevi. A well-healed linear scar on the upper back consistent with recent wide local excision site. Three palpable subcutaneous nodules along the left flank, ranging from approximately 1.0 to 1.8 cm, slightly mobile, non-tender, without overlying skin ulceration. No other suspicious lesions identified on limited skin survey today; formal full-body skin exam deferred to Dermatology.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No bony tenderness.

Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 symmetrically in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Gait steady.

---

DIAGNOSTIC DATA

Laboratory Results (obtained this visit):
- Complete Blood Count: White blood cell count 7.2 x 10³/µL (reference 4.0-10.0), hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL (mildly below reference 12.0-16.0 for females - borderline), platelets 298 x 10³/µL. Differential unremarkable.
- Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
  - BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m²
  - Glucose 98 mg/dL
  - Total protein 7.1 g/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL (low-normal)
  - AST 62 U/L (elevated; ULN ~35), ALT 48 U/L (elevated; ULN ~35)
  - Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (normal), direct bilirubin 0.3 mg/dL (normal)
  - Alkaline phosphatase 145 U/L (elevated; ULN ~120)
  - Calcium 9.2 mg/dL (normal)
- Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH): 412 U/L (elevated; ULN 171 U/L; approximately 2.4x ULN)
- TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (normal; on levothyroxine)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
- Coagulation studies: PT/INR 1.1, aPTT 29 sec (both normal)
- Hepatitis B surface antigen: Negative; hepatitis B core antibody: Negative; hepatitis C antibody: Negative
- Beta-hCG: Negative

Imaging (reviewed in preparation for this consultation):
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging): Multiple bilateral axillary lymph nodes, dominant left axillary node 4.8 x 3.5 cm with central necrosis; multiple hypodense hepatic lesions in segments IV, VI, and VII, largest 3.2 x 2.8 cm in segment VI; three subcutaneous nodules on the left flank up to 1.8 cm; no pulmonary nodules; no osseous lesions; no mediastinal adenopathy. Overall picture is consistent with widespread metastatic disease involving the liver, bilateral axillary lymph nodes, and left flank subcutaneous tissue.
- MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast (staging): No intracranial metastases, no leptomeningeal enhancement, no mass effect. Incidental 4 mm pineal cyst. Normal brain parenchyma. No evidence of CNS metastatic disease - this is reassuring.

Pathology (reviewed):
- Primary melanoma (upper back excision): Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V, primary lesion 18 mm. Margins negative after wide local excision.
- Left axillary lymph node excisional biopsy: Sheets of epithelioid melanoma cells with prominent nucleoli and frequent mitoses (>10/mm²); melanin pigment focally present. IHC: S100+, Melan-A+, SOX10+, HMB-45+, Ki-67 ~45%. BRAF V600E (VE1) strongly positive (3+). PD-L1 (22C3): TPS 30%, CPS 55. Consistent with metastatic malignant melanoma from a cutaneous primary.

Molecular / NGS (reviewed):
- 500-gene comprehensive solid tumor panel (specimen: left axillary LN metastasis, FFPE)
- BRAF p.V600E: VAF 48% - pathogenic, FDA-approved targeted therapy available
- NF1 p.Q1300* (nonsense, LOF): VAF 52% - pathogenic, RAS pathway activation
- CDKN2A homozygous deletion - pathogenic tumor suppressor loss
- TP53 p.R248W: VAF 41% - pathogenic
- PTEN p.L108R: VAF 22% - likely pathogenic
- RAC1 p.P29S: VAF 18% - likely pathogenic
- ARID2 p.E1242fs: VAF 35% - likely pathogenic
- TERT promoter C228T: VAF 55% - pathogenic (associated with aggressive behavior)
- MAP2K1 amplification (5 copies), MYC gain, CCND1 gain
- TMB: 42 mut/Mb (high)
- MSI: MSS (microsatellite stable)
- No NRAS mutation, no KIT mutation, no fusion events detected

---

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 58-year-old never-smoking female with a longstanding background of dysplastic nevus syndrome and a family history of melanoma in a first-degree relative of her mother's generation (maternal uncle), who presents with a newly diagnosed Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma arising from the back. The disease is characterized by bulky bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy (dominant left node 4.8 cm), multiple hepatic metastases (largest 3.2 cm, segments IV/VI/VII), and subcutaneous satellite nodules on the left flank. LDH is significantly elevated at 2.4x the upper limit of normal, consistent with M1c staging. There is no CNS involvement on brain MRI, which is an important favorable feature.

From a molecular standpoint, the tumor harbors a BRAF p.V600E activating mutation confirmed by both IHC and NGS (VAF 48%), which is the primary actionable driver and opens the door for BRAF-targeted therapy with a BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination. Additional co-occurring alterations include NF1 loss of function (which can contribute to RAS pathway activation as a potential resistance mechanism), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (a very common finding in melanoma), TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, TERT promoter mutation, and MAP2K1 amplification. The TMB is high at 42 mut/Mb, and PD-L1 TPS is 30% with a CPS of 55 - these features also make the tumor potentially responsive to immune checkpoint inhibition, and the combination of BRAF/MEK inhibition followed or combined with immunotherapy may ultimately be considered.

The clinical picture is one of symptomatic, rapidly progressive, bulky M1c melanoma with hepatic involvement and elevated LDH. This patient requires active and expedient systemic therapy. ECOG PS is 1. Organ function is adequate for therapy; transaminase elevation is felt to be related to hepatic metastases rather than intrinsic liver disease, and hepatitis serologies are negative.

Key decision point for initial systemic therapy: In BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, both BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination targeted therapy and upfront dual checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (e.g., ipi/Opdivo) are established first-line options with survival benefit. For this patient, several factors favor initiating BRAF/MEK inhibition (specifically, Tafinlar/Mekinist) as first-line treatment:
1. Bulky, symptomatic hepatic disease requiring rapid response - targeted therapy typically achieves faster and deeper early response compared to immunotherapy in BRAF-mutant disease
2. Significantly elevated LDH (2.4x ULN) indicating high disease burden - a population where rapid initial disease control is especially important
3. Symptomatic disease (right upper quadrant pain, constitutional symptoms) and moderate/significant weight loss indicating a patient who benefits from prompt disease control

For these reasons, we will proceed with Tafinlar (Tafinlar) 150 mg orally twice daily in combination with Mekinist (Mekinist) 2 mg orally once daily as first-line systemic therapy. This regimen is well-established based on COMBI-d and COMBI-v trial data, with response rates of approximately 66-69% and improved survival compared to single-agent BRAF inhibition. The addition of tram serves to delay the development of acquired resistance through MEK bypass mechanisms and reduces the incidence of BRAF inhibitor-associated paradoxical cutaneous side effects (including keratoacanthomas and squamous cell carcinomas).

The patient has been extensively counseled regarding the risks, benefits, and alternatives to therapy, including the option of upfront immunotherapy, which remains a reasonable alternative. She understands that targeted therapy may provide a faster initial response but that acquired resistance commonly develops, typically within 6-12 months, at which point alternative strategies including immunotherapy would be pursued.

---

PLAN

1. INITIATE Tafinlar/Mekinist TARGETED THERAPY
   - dabra (Tafinlar) 150 mg orally twice daily (approximately 12 hours apart, taken without food or with a light meal)
   - Mekinist (Mekinist) 2 mg orally once daily (taken at least 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals)
   - Start date: Planned within the next 1-2 weeks after completion of pre-treatment labs and prior authorizations.
   - Pre-treatment echocardiogram ordered to establish baseline LVEF (Mekinist is associated with cardiomyopathy in a subset of patients).
   - Ophthalmology referral placed for baseline eye exam (rare risk of retinal vein occlusion and uveitis with MEK inhibition).
   - Dermatology co-management in place given risk of new primary melanomas and cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas on BRAF inhibitor; skin checks every 3 months recommended.

2. ADVERSE EVENT COUNSELING - Patient and husband counseled in detail regarding the most common side effects of Tafinlar/tram combination therapy:
   - Pyrexia (drug fever): occurs in ~50-60% of patients on this combination. Advised to check temperature with any febrile symptom and to contact the oncology triage line for temperatures >38.5°C or fever with rigors. Protocol for dose interruption reviewed.
   - Fatigue, nausea, and headache: common, usually manageable.
   - Palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot skin reaction): moisturizing regimen pre-emptively recommended.
   - Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma / keratoacanthoma: occurs in approximately 10-15% of patients on BRAF inhibitor monotherapy, markedly reduced with MEK inhibitor combination; dermatology monitoring in place.
   - Cardiomyopathy: rare; baseline echo ordered; patients advised to report dyspnea or palpitations.
   - Hyperglycemia: rare but possible; fasting glucose checked at baseline (normal today).
   - Photosensitivity: patient advised to continue rigorous sun protection (SPF 50+, protective clothing).
   - Uveitis/blurry vision: advise to report promptly.

3. MONITORING PLAN
   - Clinic visit and labs (CBC, CMP, LDH) in 3 weeks after starting therapy.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast at approximately 8 weeks after initiation to assess treatment response.
   - Repeat brain MRI in approximately 3 months (or sooner if neurological symptoms develop).
   - Echocardiogram at baseline, then at 1 month, then every 3 months while on Mekinist.
   - Ophthalmology baseline evaluation prior to treatment start.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE
   - Nutritional support: Referral to oncology dietitian given 8 lb weight loss and decreased appetite.
   - Oncology social work referral: Patient is currently on medical leave from teaching; insurance coverage and financial assistance for medications (Tafinlar and Mekinist) to be coordinated through our specialty pharmacy team. Patient Support Solutions (manufacturer's co-pay assistance program) discussed.
   - Patient education materials regarding BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy provided.
   - Triage line number given; patient instructed to call for any fever above 38.5°C, new neurological symptoms, significant dyspnea, severe skin reaction, or other concerning symptoms.

5. GENETIC COUNSELING: Given personal history of stage IV melanoma, longstanding dysplastic nevus syndrome, and maternal uncle with cutaneous melanoma, refer to genetic counseling to evaluate for germline BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome, CDKN2A germline mutation, or other hereditary melanoma predispositions. Note that somatic CDKN2A deletion was identified on NGS but germline status unknown.

6. MULTIDISCIPLINARY DISCUSSION: Case to be presented at the Multidisciplinary Melanoma Tumor Board for collegial review of management strategy, particularly given the co-occurring NF1 loss (which may confer earlier resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition) and the potential for upfront combined targeted/immunotherapy strategies if clinical scenario evolves.

7. BRAIN MRI: Already completed at staging - negative. Repeat surveillance as above.

8. ADVANCE CARE PLANNING: Given Stage IV diagnosis, gently introduced the importance of advance directive documentation and encouraged the patient to discuss her wishes with her husband and primary care physician. Patient is not ready to discuss hospice at this stage, which is entirely appropriate given her performance status and the availability of active treatment options with meaningful response rates.

Return to Clinic: In approximately 3 weeks after treatment initiation for labs and toxicity assessment.

Electronic signature and attestation to follow.

---

Documented by: Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
Dictation reviewed and approved by attending.
